item.title: Graph-Theoretic Intrinsic Reward: Guiding RL with Effective Resistance
item.description:
BibTeX

  author={Jatin Chauhan and Shivam Bhardwaj and Aditya Saibewar and Aditya Ramesh and Sadbhavana Babar and Manohar Kaul},
  booktitle={The Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations},
  year={2026},

item.title: Efficient Jailbreak Attack Sequences on Large Language Models via Multi-Armed Bandit-based Context Switching
Fixed Budget Kernel LMS based Estimator using Random Fourier Features
Diffusion Transformer with LoRA and Action-Consistency for Robust Embodied Control
item.description:
Problem

Placeholder: embodied control under RGB perturbations and degraded visual inputs.

Why the problem is hard

Placeholder: why perception robustness and action consistency under distribution shift are difficult.

System design

Placeholder: Diffusion Transformer + LoRA + action-consistency loss → control policy.

Architecture diagram

Implementation details

Placeholder: DiT backbone, LoRA config, action-consistency formulation, perturbation augmentation.

Experiments

Placeholder: perturbation types (illumination, blur, occlusion), baselines, control metrics.

Results

Placeholder: tables or figures on robustness and stability under degraded visuals.

item.title: Reconstructing Modern Reinforcement Learning
item.description:
Reinforcement learning papers often present a misleading picture of the field. Algorithms appear as independent inventions — DQN, PPO, SAC, HER — each with its own notation, diagrams, and benchmarks. From a distance this looks like steady progress. From close up it feels like memorizing a zoo.

This series takes a different position:

Modern reinforcement learning is not a sequence of new ideas. It is a sequence of repairs.

Every major algorithm exists because a simpler learning rule failed under a specific violated assumption. Once you identify the assumption that broke, the algorithm becomes obvious. Once you don’t, the field looks mysterious.
